attributes.stem: A 34-year-old woman, G1P0, in active labour at 37+2 weeks gestation is being managed on the obstetrics ward in a rural hospital. She has diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus with good glucose control antenatally. Her BMI is 32 kg/m2. She has made good progress and her cervix is 8 cm dilated on vaginal examination 30 minutes ago. Her pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 149/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 12/min, SpO2 100% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She is quite anxious about delivering the baby and cannot stop shaking her legs. She has no abdominal pain, headaches, trouble urinating, or shortness of breath but does have 3+ lower limb reflexes with three beats of clonus. Blood tests from this morning show platelets 230 x 10^9/L (150-400), ALT 70 U/L (<55), AST 55 U/L (<45) and GGT 70 U/L (<60). The urinary protein:creatinine ratio is elevated at 37 mg/mmol (<30).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?
options: Strict fluid balance chart and fluid restriction
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with new onset pre-eclampsia in labour, with several risk factors including pre-existing diabetes mellitus, nulliparity and obesity. She has diagnostic features of pre-eclampsia: elevated blood pressure, hyperreflexia, clonus, abnormal liver function tests, and significant proteinuria (protein:creatinine ratio >30).

Intravenous magnesium sulphate infusion is the most important immediate step in severe pre-eclampsia. The standard regimen is a 4g loading dose followed by 1g per hour maintenance. Magnesium acts through multiple mechanisms: it is a calcium antagonist causing smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation, reduces cerebral vasospasm, and has membrane-stabilising properties that increase the seizure threshold. These effects help prevent eclamptic seizures and may modestly reduce blood pressure.

While transfer to a tertiary centre would be ideal, at 8 cm dilated there is a high risk of delivery during transfer, making this unsafe. The priority is stabilising the patient where she is.

Fluid balance monitoring and restriction to 80-100 mL/hour is important as these patients are at risk of pulmonary oedema, but this is not the most urgent priority.

Intravenous labetalol boluses could cause an excessive drop in blood pressure and reduce uteroplacental perfusion. Oral antihypertensives (labetalol, methyldopa or nifedipine) are preferred as first-line agents.

Ventouse delivery may eventually be indicated to avoid a prolonged second stage, but there is no immediate indication for instrumental delivery. When performed, all safety criteria must be met.
learningPoints: Severe pre-eclampsia requires immediate treatment with intravenous magnesium sulphate to prevent eclamptic seizures
Magnesium sulphate acts as both an anticonvulsant and vasodilator in pre-eclampsia
Transfer is contraindicated in advanced labour due to risk of delivery en route
references: South Australian Perinatal Practice Guideline 2020, Hypertensive disorders in Pregnancy, Department of Health, Adelaide, viewed 18/8/2020.
Townsend, R, O’brien, P & Khalil, A 2016, ‘Current best practice in the management of hypertensive disorders in pregnancy,’ Integrated Blood Pressure Control, vol. 9, pp. 79-94.

RANZCOG Guideline: Instrumental Vaginal Birth C-Obs 16 (March 2020). https://ranzcog.edu.au/RANZCOG_SITE/media/RANZCOG-MEDIA/Women%27s%20Health/Statement%20and%20guidelines/Clinical-Obstetrics/Instrumental-vaginal-birth-(C-Obs-16).pdf?ext=.pdf. Accessed 20/8/20.
